coredata.dc:title: Stimulation of surface terminating group by carbon quantum dots for improving pseudocapacitance of Ti3C2Tx MXene based electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yesheng
Cui, Yongpeng
Kong, Dongqing
Wang, Xiaoning
Li, Bin
Cai, Tonghui
Li, Xuejin
Xu, Jing
Li, Yanpeng
Yan, Youguo
Hu, Han
Wu, Mingbo
Xue, Qingzhong
Yan, Zifeng
Zhao, Lianming
Xing, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Enhancing pseudocapacitance of Ti3C2Tx MXene-based electrode materials induced by sufficient surface terminating groups (such as Ti–O bonding) becomes one promising way for fabricating high energy-density supercapacitors. However, most of terminating group activity cannot be excited well in the multilayer Ti3C2Tx due to the layer stacking feature, which limits its further performance improvement. In this paper, a CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid material is synthesized by modifying few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene matrix with highly dispersed carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In this unique hybrid model, the Ti3C2Tx MXene matrix with high conductivity and high specific surface area is just like the “paddy field”, which not only promotes the charge rapid transfer, but also provides the high double layer capacitance by ion adsorption. More importantly, the CQDs act as “rice seedlings” firmly anchored to the Ti3C2Tx matrix, and greatly stimulate the activity of the termination groups of Ti3C2Tx. As expected, this hybrid achieves a harmonious coexistence of high conductivity and high pseudocapacitance. As a result, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode delivers high reversible capacitances of 441.3 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 310.1 F g−1 at 20 A g−1, and a relatively stable cyclability with almost 100% capacitance retention after 10 000 cycles at 10 A g−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti3C2Tx MXene
Carbon quantum dot
Surface terminating group
Pseudocapacitance
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs dispersions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Taishan Scholar Foundation of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relationship between CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid structure and “paddy field” model. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis strategy for CQD@Ti3C2Tx hybrid. (b) TEM image of the f-Ti3C2Tx, the inset corresponds to the SAED pattern of f-Ti3C2Tx. (c) TEM image of the CQDs, the insets are the magnified TEM image and size distribution of CQDs. (d, f) SEM images of the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx and corresponding elemental mapping of C, Ti, O and N. (e) TEM image of the CQD@Ti3C2Tx, the insets show the SAED pattern and magnified TEM of CQD@Ti3C2Tx. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns of the prepared f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid, the inset is the magnification of XRD patterns at (002) peak. (b) Raman spectra of f-Ti3C2Tx, CQDs and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids. (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids, the inset shows pore volume distribution of all samples. (d–f) high-resolution C1s XPS, Ti 2p XPS, and O1s XPS of the f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV curves of the f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. (b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx at different current densities. (c) Gravimetric capacitance of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids at various current densities. (d) The relationship between peak current and scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1 for the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode. (e) Nyquist plot of as prepared samples, the magnified high-frequency region is shown in the inset. (f) Linear fit showing the relationship between Z′ and ω−1/2 in the low-frequency region of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode. Normalized real and imaginary capacitances of (g) f-Ti3C2Tx and (h) CQDs@Ti3C2Tx. (i) Cycling performance of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx at a current density of 10 A g−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Theoretical models of (a, b) f-Ti3C2 Tx and (c, d) CQDs@Ti3C2Tx. The magnified band structures at Fermi level of (e) f-Ti3C2Tx and (f) CQDs@Ti3C2Tx. (g) Density of states (DOS) of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx (h) The magnified DOS at Fermi level of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV curves of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx and AC electrodes at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (b) CV curves of AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx ASC at different voltage window at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (c) GCD curves at different current densities. (d) Gravimetric capacitance at various current densities for the ASC. (e) Ragone plots of the ASC. The reported MXene-based SCs values are added for comparison. (f) Cycling performance of AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx ASC at a current density of 6 A g−1 for 10 000 cycles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Stimulation of surface terminating group by carbon quantum dots for improving pseudocapacitance of Ti3C2Tx MXene based electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors Yesheng Wang and Yongpeng Cui contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhancing pseudocapacitance of Ti3C2Tx MXene-based electrode materials induced by sufficient surface terminating groups (such as Ti–O bonding) becomes one promising way for fabricating high energy-density supercapacitors. However, most of terminating group activity cannot be excited well in the multilayer Ti3C2Tx due to the layer stacking feature, which limits its further performance improvement. In this paper, a CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid material is synthesized by modifying few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene matrix with highly dispersed carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In this unique hybrid model, the Ti3C2Tx MXene matrix with high conductivity and high specific surface area is just like the “paddy field”, which not only promotes the charge rapid transfer, but also provides the high double layer capacitance by ion adsorption. More importantly, the CQDs act as “rice seedlings” firmly anchored to the Ti3C2Tx matrix, and greatly stimulate the activity of the termination groups of Ti3C2Tx. As expected, this hybrid achieves a harmonious coexistence of high conductivity and high pseudocapacitance. As a result, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode delivers high reversible capacitances of 441.3 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 310.1 F g−1 at 20 A g−1, and a relatively stable cyclability with almost 100% capacitance retention after 10 000 cycles at 10 A g−1.

A CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid is facilely designed, in which the activity of Ti–O bonding is excited well to enhance its pseudocapacitance. Consequently, this resultant CQDs@Ti3C2Tx realizes a comprehensive energy storage ability. This strategy may provide a promising approach to fabricate high-performance MXene based electrode.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs), as a kind of energy storage device different from metal ion batteries (MIBs) [1–8], are widely applied in the high power electronic devices due to their higher power density and longer cycle characteristics [9,10]. However, the less-than-ideal energy density has largely limited the widespread applications of supercapacitors in more and more occasions that require energy to be stored quickly, efficiently, and inexpensive. Therefore, substantial improvements on designing high performance electrode materials should be made to satisfy the requirements of modern supercapacitor devices. As an novel transition metal carbons/nitrides, MXene with its unique layered structure, good hydrophilicity, and excellent conductivity is widely used in electrochemical energy storage devices such as SCs and MIB [11–16].Among the many MXene materials, Ti3C2Tx MXene has been extensively studied owing to the ultra-high capacitance performance and multiple energy storage mechanisms, where the Tx represents surface terminating groups, such as Ti–O, Ti–OH, or Ti–F bonding [17–20]. The capacitance contribution of Ti3C2Tx Mxene based electrode materials is divided into two parts: the double layer capacitance provided by ion adsorption and the pseudocapacitance induced by the surface terminating group (mainly Ti–O bonding) between Ti3C2Tx layers [21–23]. Unfortunately, most of terminating group Ti–O activity cannot be excited well in the multilayer Ti3C2Tx due to the unavoidable layer stacking, which limits further enhancement of Ti3C2Tx MXene electrode capacity [22,24]. In fact, few- or monolayer Ti3C2Tx MXene is more attractive because it can expose more terminating groups and reaction sites. In addition, rational surface decoration for few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene can also induce the activation of surface terminating groups to some extent, so as to futher stimulate the pseudocapacitive behavior of Ti3C2Tx MXene electrode [25–29]. Therefore, how to synthesize few- or monolayer Ti3C2Tx MXene and futher expose/stimulate terminating groups to enhance the pseudocapacitance needs to be explored urgently.

On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with easy dispersability, high compatibility, and good chemical stability, have been extensively studied in the fields of biological imaging [30], photocatalysis [31,32], and energy conversion [33–37]. Moreover, many functional groups (such as –COOH, C–OH, C–N) on the edge of CQDs have excellent compatibility with other active materials. For example, CQDs/metal oxides (such as RuO2, MnO2, NiCo2O4, VO2) [34–37], CQDs/metal hydroxides (such as Ni(OH)2) [38], CQDs/GO and other hybrid materials [39–41], can be easily synthesized and achieve high electrochemical properties in supercapacitors or metal ion batteries. Based on this unique affinity of CQDs, expectantly, the CQDs can be used to modify the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene to construct high performance CQDs/MXene hybrid materials.

In this work, we synthesized a few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene material (denoted as f-Ti3C2Tx) using Ti3AlC2 as precursor through a series of etching, stripping and centrifugation procedures. And then, the obtained Ti3C2Tx MXene was modified with CQDs carrying –O/-N functional groups to induce more surface pseudocapacitance. The CQDs-modified Ti3C2Tx MXene hybrid (denoted as CQDs@Ti3C2Tx), is just like seedlings taking root and growing in a paddy field (Fig. 1 ). In this model, the Ti3C2Tx MXene matrices with high conductivity and high specific surface area are considered as the “paddy field”, which not only promote the charge rapid transfer, but also provide the high double layer capacitance by ion adsorption. Meanwhile, the CQDs are like “rice seedlings”, which is firmly rooted in the Ti3C2Tx “paddy field” matrix through Ti–O–C covalent bonding, so as to capture more electrolyte-ions and induce pseudocapacitance. Thanks to this unique composting structure, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid can let independent Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs do their best, which achieves a harmonious coexistence of high conductivity and high pseudocapacitance. Therefore, this CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid are expected to be an advanced electrode material with a comprehensive performance for supercapacitor.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the overall synthesis processes of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid through a self-assembly process of few layer Ti3C2Tx and carbon quantum dots. In the typical preparing process, the few layer Ti3C2Tx dispersion (f-Ti3C2Tx) is first prepared by etching and stripping bulk Ti3AlC2 using a mixed solution of HCl and LiF. As shown in Fig. S1a and b, the bulk Ti3AlC2 materials was etched and exfoliated to form a single crystalline lamellar sheet with lateral sizes up to a few hundred nanometers (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1c-d). These laminate sheets provide a high specific surface area and high crystallinity. Notably, during the exfoliating process, a large number of terminating groups were grafted on the surface of f-Ti3C2Tx, which can be used as active sites to anchor active material to the f-Ti3C2Tx matrix material. At the same time, carbon quantum dots dispersion (CQDs) platelets were obtained by hydrothermal treatment in 2% acetic acid solution using chitosan as carbon source. As shown in Fig. 2c and S2a-c, the sizes of CQDs are mostly concentrated between 2 and 4 nm, and the (002) crystal plane with 0.345 nm interlayer spacing is clearly visible [33,36]. The f-Ti3C2Tx MXene sheets, with abundant terminating groups, react with the CQDs platelets to form covalently cross-linked CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid via a typical self-assembly process.

Fig. 2d and e are the SEM and TEM images of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid. This hybrid still maintains the few layers skeleton structure, and the closely arranged CQDs platelets can be seen on the Ti3C2 skeleton matrix. The structures of other two hybrids (Fig. S1e-f) are similar to that of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx. Moreover, from high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in inset of Fig. 1e, the several nanometer sized CQDs with obvious (002) crystal plane still can be clearly visible. And the existence of diffraction spots of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid proves the integrity of the f-Ti3C2Tx crystal matrix during the self-assembly process. Thanks to the attraction of marginal terminating groups of f-Ti3C2Tx MXene sheets, this unique hybrid model is just like seedlings taking root in a paddy field through the Ti–O–C covalent bonding at the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx heterointerface, thereby reinforcing the linking of the CQDs and f-Ti3C2Tx sheets and increasing charge transport. The elemental mapping spectra show uniform distribution of N element into the f-Ti3C2Tx MXene matrix (Fig. 2f), which further confirm that the CQDs platelets have been successfully introduced within the f-Ti3C2Tx MXene matrix.

The crystal structure of the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid is also investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As showed in Fig. 3 a, most of the strong diffraction peaks of Ti3AlC2 precursor (Fig S3) disappeared after etching and exfoliating [42,43]. Besides, the XRD patterns of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid exhibit a intensifying left-shift for (002) peak in contrast to that of f-Ti3C2Tx MXene with the increase of CQDs content (inset of Fig. 3a), further proving that the introduction of CQDs can increase the interlayer spacing of the f-Ti3C2Tx MXene. These results are consistent with the Raman spectroscopy analysis in Fig. 3b. The continuous characteristic peaks are observed located at ∼197, ∼282, ∼359, ∼566, and ∼604 cm−1 for f-Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid, which correspond to the A1g and Eg vibrational modes [21,44–46]. It is worth noting that one diffraction peak located at ∼156 cm−1 appears in the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid, but not detected in the pure CQD sample and pure f-Ti3C2Tx MXene sample [47,48]. This diffraction peak corresponds to the Ti–O vibrational modes, indicating the introduction of CQDs induces more exposure of Ti–O bond. At the same time, the intensity of two peaks located at ∼1355 and ∼1580 cm−1 for CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid, corresponding to the disordered-induced D-band and graphite G-band respectively, is significantly enhanced compared that of f-Ti3C2Tx which also proves the successful introduction of CQDs into f-Ti3C2Tx MXene [44,45]. Moreover, there is an observed blue shift of ∼11 cm−1 from CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-0.5 (∼1577 cm−1), CQDs@Ti3C2Tx (∼1580 cm−1), to CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-2 (∼1588 cm−1), which is mainly because of the strong Ti–O–C binding between CQDs and f-Ti3C2Tx MXene (inset of Fig. 3b). In addition, the intensity ratio values of the D band and G band (ID/IG) for CQDs and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids are shown in Table S1. In contrast to that of the pure CQDs, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids have a lower values of ID/IG, indicating a higher-degree of graphitization in the hybrids. According to previously reports [49], the Ti3C2Tx MXene has a well reduction ability, which could reduce CQDs. And in this reduction process, Ti(II or III)-based surface functional groups may transformed into Ti(IV)-based active sites such as Ti–O group.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were investigated to analyze the specific surface area and porosity structure of all samples. As shown in Fig. 3c, the CQDs platelets can be inserted into the interlayer of f-Ti3C2Tx MXene during the self-assembly process, which can prevent the agglomeration of f-Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, thus improving the specific surface area and pore structure of the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx materials (Table S2). However, too many CQDs platelets are overlaid on the surface of f-Ti3C2Tx MXene, so as to block the transmission channel, which results in the detecting difficulty of the pores inside f-Ti3C2Tx MXene. This result can also be confirmed by the HRTEM image (Fig. 2e).

The XPS and FTIR spectra were performed to analyze the chemical bonding states of the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid. The XPS survey spectra indicate the presence of C, O, Ti, and N elements in the obtained hybrid (Fig. S4a). Fig. 3d–f demonstrate the high-resolution C 1s, Ti 2p, and O 1s XPS spectra. For the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of f-Ti3C2Tx sample, four characteristic peaks were observed centered at 281.8 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.2 eV, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to C–Ti, CC/C–C, C–O/C–N, and –COOR bonding, respectively [50,51]. Notably, a characteristic peak with a 286.4 eV binding energy is observed for CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid, which is attributed to C–O–Ti bonding [52]. Correspondingly, in the high-resolution O1s spectrum, an additional peak centered at 531 eV is assigned to not only the CO bonding but also the C–O–Ti bonding [53,54]. The above results can also be confirmed by the FTIR spectra of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid (Fig. S5) [52,55]. At the same time, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum exhibits an obvious peak intensity enhancement of C–Ti-Ox and C–Ti-(OH)x bonding, implying more exposure of terminating groups under the induction action of CQDs platelets [24]. Besides, the introduction of N-containing functional group along with CQDs platelets can produce more active sites to provide more pseudocapacitance (Fig. S4b). Based on these results, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid is expected to achieve a comprehensive electrochemical performance.

The electrochemical performance of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids were tested using a three-electrode system with 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution as electrolyte. Fig. 5 a shows the CV curve of the prepared samples tested at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Due to the multiple charge storage mechanisms in the electrodes, all the curves show a deviation from an ideal “capacitor-like” rectangular shape. There are a pair of broad redox peaks from −0.5 to −0.3 V in all CV curves, which indicates the pseudocapacitive charge storage mechanism considering the reversible redox-activity of protons with the Ti–O terminating group of Ti3C2Tx [21,22]. It's worth noting that the CV profile for CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids has a larger area range compared with f-Ti3C2Tx, indicating that it has more charge storage capacitance. More importantly, the intensity of redox peaks from −0.5 to −0.3 V of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids is obviously strengthened with the introduction of CQD, which indicates that the enhanced capacitance mainly comes from the pseudocapacitance provided by CQDs that generate more Ti–O functional group in the Ti3C2Tx matrix. In addition, this pair of broad redox peaks of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids remained visible without obvious distortion with the increase of scan rates from 5 to 50 mV s−1, indicating the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids have good rate performance (Figs. S6b-d). The galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids samples were tested at various current densities from 1 to 20 A g−1. As we can seen from Fig. 4b and S7, all galvanostatic charge-discharge curves are symmetrical with a slight distortion, which shows that all electrodes have good reversibility and high coulombic efficiency. As is shown in Fig. 4c, the specific capacitances of f-Ti3C2Tx, CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-0.5, CQDs@Ti3C2Tx, and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-2 are 277 F g−1, 398.7 F g−1, 441.3 F g−1, and 386.7 F g−1, respectively, at a current density of 1 A g−1. The capacitance value attenuation of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-2 electorde may be due to the decrease of specific surface area and porosity. When the current density increases to 20 A g−1, the corresponding capacity retention are 54.0%, 67.4%, 70.3%, and 69.1%, respectively. Such high electrochemical performance is comparable with the previously reported MXene-based electrode (Table S3). Therefore, the above results indicate that the modification of Ti3C2Tx electrode by CQDs induces the generation of more pseudocapacitance.

To deeply explore the charge storage mechanism of the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode, the CV curves at different scan rates are analyzed. The relationship between the peak current (i) and the scan rate (v) could be well explained based on the following power-law: i = av
b , where a and b are adjustable parameters [56]. The b-value could be acquired by calculating the slope of the log (v)−log (i) plot. When b-value is 0.5, the charge storage is a solid diffusion-controlled process, while a b-value of 1.0 corresponds to a surface-controlled process. As shown in Fig. 4d, the b values of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode is calculated to be 0.91/0.90 at the anodic/cathodic peaks based on the slope of fitted lines, indicating the superiority of surface-controlled process and fast electrode kinetics.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the various electrode were conducted in order to better understand the process of electron and ion transfer. Fig. 4e exhibits the Nyquist plots with a frequency range from 100 kHz to 10 mHz. The inset of Fig. 4e is a local magnified image of high-frequency region. Calculated by using the equivalent circuit model (Fig. S8), all electrode show a relatively low value of the equivalent series resistance (Rs, ∼1.0 Ω) at high frequency (Table S4), which implies that the f-Ti3C2Tx matrix has good electrical conductivity. It's worth noting that the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode shows a lower value of charge transfer resistance (Rct, ∼0.246 Ω) than that of f-Ti3C2Tx electrode (∼0.756 Ω) at intermediate frequency region, which indicates that the modification of CQDs promotes the charge transfer in f-Ti3C2Tx matrix (Table S4). Moreover, in the low frequency region, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode shows a high slope angle with much closer to the real axis, suggesting a better capacitive behavior and smaller ion diffusion resistance. These results indicate that the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode has high charge transfer speed, and it can be further verified by the relationship between Z′ and ω (ω = 2πf) and a capacitance C (ω) model. As shown in Fig. 4f, CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode has a lower slope in the plots of Z′ against ω
−1/2 , illuminating that it possesses low ion diffusion coefficient [46]. Generally, the C (ω) could be divided into two parts denoted by real part C′ (ω) and imaginary part C′′ (ω). The normalized C′ (ω) of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode exhibits a slower declining trend than that of f-Ti3C2Tx electorde with the increase of frequency. And the operating frequency (f o) of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode, at which the capacitance is 50% of its maximum value, can reach up to 0.316 Hz, corresponding to the relaxation time constant (τ o) of 3.16 s. This value is pretty higher than that of f-Ti3C2Tx electrode (f o = 0.12 Hz, and τ o = 8.26 s), demonstrating the superior charge transport property. Therefore, the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode will be highly applicable for supercapacitor. We tested the cycle stability of supercapacitor based on CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode to demonstrate its suitability for practical applications. From Fig. 4i, the supercapacitor is almost no capacitance deterioration after 10 000 cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1, evidencing the excellent cycling stability.

In order to reveal the relationship between electrochemical performance and physicochemical properties of electrode materials, the interfacial properties of f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids are studied by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The theoretical models of the f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrids are exhibited in Fig. 5a–d. The carbon quantum dots are connected with Ti3C2Tx by Ti–O–C bond in CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid. In Fig. 5e and f, it can be observed that several energy bands through Fermi level, indicating the existence of the electron in Fermi level, and suggesting that both f-Ti3C2Tx and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx are conductor. Figs. S11a-c shows the partial density of states (PDOS) diagrams of each sample. It can be found that the density of states near the Fermi level of f-Ti3C2Tx is mainly contributed by both the p and d orbitals, which indicates that the charge transfer mainly occurs on Ti/O atoms [57]. Obviously, the p orbitals PDOS at Fermi level for CQDs@Ti3C2Tx is well enhanced considering the incorporation of CQDs (Fig. S10b). As a result, the total density of states of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx closing to Fermi level is remarkable increased, with the electron concentration of 22.19 electrons/eV (Fig. 5g and h), which helps to facilitate the charge transfer and induces more pseudocapacitance.

Based on the superior electrochemical performance of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode, AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx asymmetrical supercapacitor (ASC) is assembled in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte using CQDs@Ti3C2Tx as the negative electrode and activated carbon as the positive electrode to explore the potential for practical application. The electrochemical property analysis of AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx ASC are shown in Fig. 6 . The voltage window of the ASC device is attempted from 0 to 1.0 to 0–1.6 V, based on the different operating potential of positive and negative electorde at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (Fig. 6a and b). As shown in Fig. 6b, the optimal voltage window without significant polarization of device is in the 0–1.4 V range. Based on the discharging time, the specific capacitance of the ASC device is calculated at various current densities from 0.5 to 20 A g−1 (Fig. 6c). The device shows a specific capacitance of 94.4 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and the capacitance retention rate to 63.7% at 20 A g−1, which shows an excellent electrochemical performance (Fig. 6d). The energy/power density information of the AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx ASC device is shown in Fig. 6e. The device exhibits a high energy density of 25.8 Wh kg−1 at power density of 350 W kg−1. When at power density of 14 000 W kg−1, this device can still retain a high energy density of 16.4 Wh kg−1. These results are superior or comparable to previously reported MXene-based supercapacitor [46,50,58–62]. Furthermore, thanks to the excellent cycling capability of CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode, the assembled AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx ASC device holds a high capacitance retention of 96.2% after 10 000 cycles (Fig. 6f), demonstrating a superior cycling performance.

In summary, we constructed a CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid material. In this hybrid model, the Ti3C2Tx MXene matrices with high conductivity and high specific surface area can not only promote the charge rapid transfer, but also provide the high double layer capacitance by ion adsorption. More importantly, under the induction of CQDs, more and more edge terminating groups (especially the Ti–O bonding) of Ti3C2Tx MXene were activated, so as to capture more electrolyte-ions and induce pseudocapacitance. As a result, CQDs@Ti3C2Tx electrode showed satisfactory electrochemical performances, with a specific capacity of 441.3F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and high capacity retention of 70.3% at 20 A g−1. Based on this electrode, the assembled AC//CQDs@Ti3C2Tx asymmetrical supercapacitor delivers a high energy density of 25.8 Wh kg−1 at power density of 350 W kg−1. Therefore, this work may offer a particular strategy for rational design of high capacity MXene-based electrode materials for energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: Ti3AlC2 powder was commercially purchased from 11 Technology Co. Ltd. The few-layer nanosheets were prepared according to the previous study [63]. Firstly, 0.99 g LiF was completely dissolved in 20 mL HCl (12 M) to form a homogeneous solution. Then 1 g Ti3AlC2 powder was slowly added to the above mixed solution followed by continuously stirring for 24 h at 35 °C. The obtained product was washed and centrifuged for several times with deionized water until the pH was about 6. Subsequently, the powder was dispersed in 20 mL alcohol solution followed by sonication treatment for 1 h. The above mixture was centrifuged to remove the alcohol. And then the intercalated Ti3C2Tx MXene was acquired. The intercalated Ti3C2Tx MXene was redispersed to 60 mL deionized water under sonication for 20 min. Finally, a uniform few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene solution with nominal concentration of 5 mg mL−1 was collected after centrifuging.

The preparation process of CQDs is as follows [64]. 2 g of chitosan was dissolved in 18 mL 2% acetic acid solution. After, the mixture was poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclave with a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling down naturally, the dark brown solution was centrifuged to remove impurities. As a result, a bright yellow CQDs was dispersed to deionized water dispersion to form a uniform solution with a concentration of 4 mg mL−1.

Firstly, Ti3C2Tx MXene dispersion and CQDs solution was diluted using deionized water to 2 mg mL−110 mL of CQDs solution was dropped into 10 mL Ti3C2Tx MXene dispersion under stirring for 30 min. Then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL PTFE reactor followed by injecting Ar gas into the solution for 15 min for avoid the over oxidation of Ti3C2Tx MXene in the process of reaction. After that, the mixture was sealed in a stainless-steel autoclave and placed in an oven with a hydrothermal process at 180 °C for 6 h. Afterwards, the precipitate was washed for several times and vacuum filtrated followed by freeze drying. The as-received product was denoted as CQDs@Ti3C2Tx. The other two hybrids could be denoted as CQDs@ Ti3C2Tx-x, where x is the mass ratio between CQDs and Ti3C2Tx. CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-0.5 and CQDs@Ti3C2Tx-2 could be obtained via changing the volume of CQDs suspension with 2.5 and 10 mL, respectively. For comparison, pure Ti3C2Tx MXene powder was prepared through freeze drying directly without further treatment, which was named as f-Ti3C2Tx.

The electrochemical performances were measured in three-electrode system in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte with the YP-50 (Shenshenlicai Co. Ltd) as a counter electrode and Hg/HgSO4 as a reference electrode. To prepare the working electrode, the prepared samples were mixed with 5% PTFE solution in a weight ratio of 95:5. And then the slurry was coated onto a 1 cm∗1 cm graphite paper. The prepared electrodes were vacuum dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The mass loading of active material is about 2 mg. All electrochemical experiments were carried out with a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were obtained in the voltage range between 0.2 and −0.55 V at a scan rate from 5 to 200 mV s−1. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements were conducted at various densities from 1 to 20 A g−1. Electrochemical impedance (EIS) spectra were tested in frequencies ranged from 10 mHz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 10 mV. The specific capacitance (Cs) can be calculated based on the following formula:
(1) Cs=I∗Δt/(m∗ΔV)
where Cs is the specific capacitance of the working electrode, I (A) is the input current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, m (g) is mass of active material in the electrode, and ΔV (V) is the voltage window.

An asymmetric electrochemical cell, in which CQDs@Ti3C2Tx hybrid was used as the negative electrode and AC as positive electrode, was fabricated in 1 M H2SO4. The following equation was used for making sure the mass of the two electrodes is balanced according to the charge balancing criterion.
(2)
q = m∗Cs∗ΔV

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of asymmetric capacitors are calculated according to the following formula.
(3) E = 0.5∗Cs∗ΔV2/3.6
(4)
P = E/Δt
in which m is the total mass of both electrodes, and Δt represents the discharge time. The real part C′ (ω) and imaginary part C″ (ω) capacitances is calculated by the following formulas.
(5) C′(ω) = Z′(ω)/(ω∗|Z(ω)|2)
(6) C′(ω) = Z′(ω)/(ω∗|Z(ω)|2)
where ω stands for the angular frequency, Z′ (ω) is the real part of the Z (ω), and Z″ (ω) is the imaginary parts of the Z (ω).

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Germany) with monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm, with a scan speed of 5 min−1) was used to detect the crystalline structures of samples. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured by a Micrometrics ASAP 2020 analyzer. All samples were degassed at 100 °C for 12 h for further testing. The surface area was calculated according to the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation. And the nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT) method was used to analyze the pore-size distributions of the prepared samples. The microstructure and morphology of the material were conducted by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7900F, JEOL, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, Tecnai G2 20 STWIN). The surface properties were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, COUNTRY) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI5000 Versa Probe, ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The Raman spectra were measured by Electronic Raman spectroscope (DXR, Thermo Fisher Scientific).

The Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package (CASTEP) in Materials Studio (MS) was used to study the density of states (DOS) and band structure. A 3 × 3 × 1 supercell of Ti3C2Tx (T = O) was established, labelled as f-Ti3C2Tx. For the CQDs@Ti3C2Tx structure, Ti3C2Tx was anchored by CQDs containing 16 C atoms and other atoms via Ti–O–C bond. Geometry optimization and electronic structure calculations were performed using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) to describe the exchange and correlation effects [65]. Furthermore, Grimme's PBE + D2 method was used to consider the long-range dispersion forces, which shows a comparable reliability with the PBE0 hybrid XC functional including an ab initio van der Waals correction [66,67]. A plane wave basis set and norm-conserving pseudopotentials were used as implemented in the CASTEP program [68,69]. The electronic wave functions were expanded in a plane wave basis with a cutoff energy of 680 eV. Based on the Monkhorst–Pack (MP) method, the Brillouin zone was sampled using 4 × 4 × 1 k-points used for structure optimization and 8 × 8 × 1 k-points adopted for calculations of electronic properties. The convergence criteria for force, stress, and energy variation were set to 0.03 eV/Å, 0.05 GPa, and 1 × 10−5 eV/atom.

Yesheng Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yongpeng Cui: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Dongqing Kong: Writing – original draft. Xiaoning Wang: Writing – original draft. Bin Li: Writing – original draft. Tonghui Cai: Writing – review & editing. Xuejin Li: Writing – review & editing. Jing Xu: Writing – review & editing. Yanpeng Li: Investigation. Youguo Yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Han Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mingbo Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qingzhong Xue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zifeng Yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lianming Zhao: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Wei Xing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51877216, 21805307), Taishan Scholar Foundation (tsqn20161017), Major Program of Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR201801280009), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB078), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (18 C × 05007 A, 20 C × 06101 A, 19 C × 05001 A, 19 C × 05002 A), the PetroChina Innovation Foundation (2018D-5007-0504), and the Postdoctoral Applied Research Program of Qingdao.
